Chapter named in honor of Benjamin Fenn

emigration.notes: Likely arrived 1637 with a group from Buckinghamshire — NOT on the 1630 Mary and John as sometimes stated online. First appears in colonial records 1637; seven-year gap makes 1630 arrival implausible. Removed from Dorchester to Milford where he received lot #3 (2 acres 11 rods 3 poles).
emigration.discrepancy_note: Online sources give 1630 / Mary and John. Genealogical blog (Empty Branches) argues compellingly for 1637 Buckinghamshire group. 1637 preferred.

narrative_blocks.header: Milford magistrate and a colonial Assistant
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Fenn was a deacon, magistrate, and militia officer at Milford. He represented the town in the New Haven Colony and served as an Assistant of the colony from 1665 to 1672.

narrative_blocks.header: He took lot number three at Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Stewkley in Buckinghamshire, Fenn came over in the Great Migration and settled at Milford. He held land on both sides of the Atlantic for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Martha married Samuel Newton, a grandson of Thomas Hooker; his daughter Susanna married William Hooker of Farmington. Both ties pull the Fenn family into the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left each grandchild a single ewe
narrative_blocks.body: Fenn's 1672 will divided farms in Milford, New Haven, and Buckinghamshire among his sons. To every one of his grandchildren he left a single ewe sheep.

career.notes: Milford deputy 1653; magistrate 1654-1663; Commissioner of the United Colonies 1661 and 1663.

narrative_blocks.header: Milford magistrate and a colonial Assistant
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Fenn was a deacon, magistrate, and militia officer at Milford. He represented the town in the New Haven Colony and served as an Assistant of the colony from 1665 to 1672.

narrative_blocks.header: He took lot number three at Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Stewkley in Buckinghamshire, Fenn came over in the Great Migration and settled at Milford. He held land on both sides of the Atlantic for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Martha married Samuel Newton, a grandson of Thomas Hooker; his daughter Susanna married William Hooker of Farmington. Both ties pull the Fenn family into the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left each grandchild a single ewe
narrative_blocks.body: Fenn's 1672 will divided farms in Milford, New Haven, and Buckinghamshire among his sons. To every one of his grandchildren he left a single ewe sheep.

career.notes: Milford deputy 1653; magistrate 1654-1663; Commissioner of the United Colonies 1661 and 1663.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Peter Prudden's garden, Milford Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father died during the Atlantic crossing
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Baldwin sailed for New England in 1638 aboard the Martin. Her father, Sylvester Baldwin, made his will on the ocean and died during the crossing, when she was about seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children, then buried in Prudden's garden
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah married Benjamin Fenn at Milford and bore six children. She died in 1663 and was buried in Peter Prudden's garden at Milford.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Baldwin, bapt. April 22, 1621, Aston Clinton, Buckinghamshire, England
parents.father_research_notes: Benjamin Fenn, b. ca. 1612 Stewkley, Buckinghamshire, England. Emigrated ca. 1630. Assistant Governor of Connecticut 1665-1672. Stewkley is in the same county as Amersham, where Thomas Hooker married Susanna Garbrand on April 3, 1621.

cross_connections.display_label: Her sister married the merchant across from his father's store.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father was Connecticut Assistant Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Benjamin Fenn emigrated from Stewkley, Buckinghamshire, England — the same county where Rev. Thomas Hooker had married Susanna Garbrand in 1621. Fenn served as Assistant Governor of Connecticut from 1665 to 1672. Martha's half-sister Susanna Fenn married William Hooker — bringing the Fenn family into the Hooker tree twice.

burial.plot_notes: Oldest part of the Milford Cemetery. FindAGrave #29848117.

cross_connections.display_label: Their wives were half-sisters — Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Militia ensign in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: When King Philip's War broke out in 1675, Samuel was appointed ensign in the Milford militia at roughly age 29. He served through the war and its recurring aftermath of Indian skirmishes, rising to captain of the Milford militia by 1698.

narrative_blocks.header: Represented Milford fifteen times in the General Court
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Milford in fifteen sessions of the Connecticut General Court between 1691 and 1703 — a span of sustained civic service that ran alongside his captaincy of the militia. His father Roger Newton Sr. had helped found the town's church.

narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker left him £10 in his will
narrative_blocks.body: He was nine months old when his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker died in 1647. Thomas's will left a £10 legacy to him — a substantial inheritance for a Hooker grandchild born during the Hartford founding.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife and cousin's wife were Fenn half-sisters
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Martha Fenn's half-sister Susanna married William Hooker — Samuel's first cousin. The Fenn family thus connected to the Hooker tree twice in the same generation through different lines.

narrative_blocks.header: A second marriage to a soldier's widow
narrative_blocks.body: After Martha Fenn died, Samuel married Sarah Welch Fowler, widow of John Fowler, who had been killed taking an Indian fort in 1693. Their son Abner bore a name found nowhere else in the Newton or Hooker line.

career.notes: Represented Milford in 15 sessions of the General Court, 1691-1703.

military_service.notes: Appointed ensign 1675 at outbreak of King Philip's War. Served in recurring skirmishes with Indians. Rose to captain of Milford militia 1698. Source: Newton Genealogy (Leonard 1915).

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Constable of New London 1680

narrative_blocks.header: Whole family entered the church covenant together
narrative_blocks.body: On April 1, 1711, Samuel and Phebe were admitted to full communion in the Milford church — on the very same day their two eldest children, Phebe and Samuel, were baptized. A single Sunday that sealed the whole family into the covenant at once.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Newton cousins married two Platt sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Phebe Platt in Milford; his first cousin Fletcher Newton married Phebe's sister Hannah Platt. Two Newton cousins took two Platt sisters — the families of Milford interlacing through a single generation.

cross_connections.display_label: His first cousin — both married Platt sisters from the same Milford family.

children_summary.notes: No stars in Edward Hooker despite distinguished career — likely incomplete records for this branch. Birth dates for Caroline Newton children all required correction as given dates predate parents' 1712 marriage.

political_offices.title: Judge, County Court
political_offices.discrepancy_note: EH gives 34 years; Families of Early Milford gives 33 years. Unresolved.

political_offices.title: Chief Judge
political_offices.discrepancy_note: EH gives 34 years; Families of Early Milford gives 33 years. Unresolved.

sources.notes: Entry 13.5; birth dates for several children required correction

burial.plot_notes: Buried next to wife Susannah. Tombstone inscription preserved below.

narrative_blocks.header: He commanded Connecticut’s troops at Louisbourg
narrative_blocks.body: Commander of Connecticut troops at the Siege of Louisbourg 1745 — capture of French fortress at Cape Breton Island, one of most significant colonial military victories

narrative_blocks.header: Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment
narrative_blocks.body: Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment

narrative_blocks.header: Chief Judge of County Court 1737-1771 — 34 years
narrative_blocks.body: Chief Judge of County Court 1737-1771 — 34 years

narrative_blocks.header: Assistant of Colony of Connecticut 1738-1771 — 33 years
narrative_blocks.body: Assistant of Colony of Connecticut 1738-1771 — 33 years

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard graduate; Captain in Queen Anne's War 1709
narrative_blocks.body: Harvard graduate; Captain in Queen Anne's War 1709

narrative_blocks.header: “Newton as steel inflexible from right”
narrative_blocks.body: His Milford epitaph closes in verse: “The part the Hero left beneath the skies / Newton as steel inflexible from right / In Faith, in Law, in Equity, in Fight.” He had been thirty-three years a judge and an Assistant of the colony when he died in his eighty-seventh year.

education.notes: Harvard graduate; year not recorded in sources

military_service.role: Commander of Connecticut troops at Siege of Louisbourg
military_service.notes: New England forces captured the French fortress at Cape Breton Island without British regular army support. One of most significant colonial military victories. Many years Colonel of the Second Connecticut Regiment.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.bio_blurb: Louisbourg commander, judge, Assistant thirty-three years

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter married Judge James Hooker of Guilford
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Fenn was a daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford. Her daughter Mary Leete married James Hooker, a grandson of Thomas Hooker and the first probate judge of the Guilford district.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried a King William’s War captain
narrative_blocks.body: After William Leete died in 1687, Mary married Captain Stephen Bradley of Guilford, who served as an officer of dragoons and a trainband captain during King William’s War.

open_questions: Is wife Mary Fenn a daughter of Benjamin Fenn (X00010)? X00010 records list "Mary (bapt 1647)" among children of first marriage to Sarah Baldwin. If confirmed, this creates a Fenn cross-connection through the Leete line.

sources.notes: Mary identified as "only daughter of William and Mary (Fenn) Leete"

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her 1674 will planned a return to England
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Fenn’s second wife, Susannah Ward, outlived him and in her 1674 will declared her intent to sail home to Worcester, England, where her aged mother was still living.

sources.notes: Birth May 4, 1669; daughter of Benjamin Fenn and Susanna Wade

narrative_blocks.header: Her first husband died leaving her pregnant
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker died in 1689. Susanna gave birth to their daughter Susannah in June 1690 — born into a fatherless household. She then remarried John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna was a daughter of Benjamin Fenn by his second wife, Susanna Wade, and half-sister to Martha Fenn Newton. She married William Hooker of Farmington in the 1680s.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed when William Hooker died in 1689, Susanna married John Blackleach, a prominent Hartford merchant. She died about 1698.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife's sister married the Farmington minister he succeeded.
Their mothers were sisters — the Fenn family ran through both.
His father-in-law — patriarch of the Fenn family.

narrative_blocks.header: Store at Farmington's most prominent corner
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker's store stood at the most prominent corner in Farmington — opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge. He died at 26, leaving only a daughter, Susannah, who was named in her grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker's will.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Fenn sisters married three prominent men
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Susanna Fenn was one of three Fenn sisters who married into prominent Connecticut families in the same generation. Susanna married him; her sister Martha married Samuel Newton; her sister Mary married William Leete Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-six with one infant daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He died in 1689 at age 26 — his will dated August 8 with inventory totaling £364-11-06. He left wife Susanna and an infant daughter Susannah; Susanna remarried John Blackleach within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Rev. Samuel Hooker, grandson of the founder
narrative_blocks.body: William was a son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington and a grandson of Thomas Hooker. His store stood across the road from his father's house.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow married the merchant John Blackleach
narrative_blocks.body: After William died in 1689, his widow Susanna Fenn married John Blackleach, a noted Hartford merchant. Their infant daughter Susannah carried the Hooker line forward.

career.notes: Store at the most prominent corner in Farmington, opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge.

narrative_blocks.header: He came to hold William Hooker’s estate
narrative_blocks.body: A London-born merchant’s son, John Blackleach married the widow of William Hooker, and Connecticut probate records show the Hooker estate passing into his keeping before his death in 1703.

death.cause: childbirth complications (died one month after daughter born Sept 3, 1709)

narrative_blocks.header: Born after her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father William Hooker died in 1689; she was born June 8, 1690, after his death. Her mother Susanna Fenn Blackleach died around 1698 — leaving Susannah an orphan at approximately eight years old. She was raised in the Farmington household through the end of the seventeenth century.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in childbirth at nineteen
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Susannah was born on September 3, 1709. On October 5, 1709 — barely one month later — she died in Stamford at age 19. The cause is almost certainly childbirth complications.

sources.notes: James Fenn s of Benjamin and Susanna (Wade); b. May 14, 1672

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into the Hooker–Stone line
narrative_blocks.body: James Fenn was a son of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford. His daughter Joanna married John Stone, a great-grandson of Thomas Hooker.

parents.mother_research_notes: Grace Judson (second wife of Samuel Prudden X00103)

sources.notes: Joanna Prudden bpt May 7, 1676; d. June 2, 1740 ae 64 (TSM)
Joanna bpt 1716, d. May 19, 1741 ae 25 (TSM); mar Oct 2, 1740 John Stone s of Ezekiel & Hannah (Merwin)

burialCemetery.name: Milford Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes:
Active burial ground for Milford founding families. Mary Hooker Newton (H00003) buried here in what was historically called 'Peter Prudden's Garden' — no tombstone survives for her. John Newton (H00014), Lydia Ford Newton (I00012), Samuel Newton (H00011), and Ezekiel Newton (H00015) also buried here. Prudden was first minister of Milford.
GPS from FindAGrave (H00011 Samuel Newton burial).
